title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Spiritual Teachers
meta_description: Trust your gut when evaluating spiritual teachers. This guide shows you how to use intuition, not fear, to spot red flags and protect your energy.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Spiritual Teachers
og_description: Trust your gut when evaluating spiritual teachers. This guide shows you how to use intuition, not fear, to spot red flags and protect your energy.
body:
You've Been Burned Before. Here's How to Trust Yourself Again.

Let me guess. You found someone who seemed like the real deal. They spoke with such certainty. They looked at you like they could see straight through to your soul. And you thought, finally, someone who gets it. Someone who can help me untangle all this mess.

Then something shifted. A comment that felt off. A request for money that didn't sit right. The way they talked about other teachers with a little too much venom. And suddenly that warm feeling in your chest turned cold. You felt stupid. Naive. Like you'd been played.

You're not stupid. You're sensitive. And sensitive people are drawn to light - even when that light is coming from a place that's not quite what it seems. The problem isn't that you trusted. The problem is that you didn't know how to use your own intuition to check whether that trust was earned.

So let's fix that. Right now.

Why Your Gut Already Knows What Your Brain Won't Accept

Here's the thing about intuition when it comes to spiritual teachers: your nervous system picks up on things your conscious mind misses. You might feel a slight tightening in your chest when they say something. A flicker of unease. A sense that the energy in the room just dropped a few degrees. But then your brain jumps in and says, "But they said such beautiful things! They seem so enlightened! I must be projecting my own stuff onto them."

Sound familiar? Right?!

That's the trap. You've been trained to override your own signals. Especially if you grew up in a home where your feelings were dismissed or minimized. You learned to second-guess yourself before you even knew what second-guessing was. And spiritual communities can be a perfect breeding ground for that same dynamic - because the teacher is supposed to be "evolved" and you're supposed to be "working on yourself."

But here's what nobody tells you: your intuition doesn't need to be perfect. It just needs to be heard. You don't have to act on every flicker of doubt. But you do have to stop pretending those flickers don't exist.

"Your intuition is not the enemy of your spiritual growth. It's the guardian at the gate. And it's been trying to get your attention for a long time."

The First Red Flag: When They Claim to Have All the Answers

Any teacher who tells you they have the complete map, the final truth, the only path - run. I'm not kidding. Real spiritual growth is messy, individual, and deeply personal. No single person holds the keys to your soul. If someone presents themselves as the ultimate authority, they're building a cage, not a doorway.

Your intuition will often pick up on this as a kind of pressure. Not the gentle push of a good teacher who challenges you, but a heavy, "you must do this or you'll miss out" kind of energy. Does that land?

When you feel that pressure, stop. Breathe. Ask yourself: "Does this person leave room for me to disagree? For me to have my own experience? For me to walk away?" If the answer is no, even in a subtle way, that's your intuition waving a red flag.

I've seen so many sensitive people get hooked by teachers who offered certainty. Because when you're feeling lost and overwhelmed, certainty feels like a life raft. But the problem is, that raft often comes with chains attached. A good teacher helps you find your own certainty. A problematic one makes you dependent on theirs.

How Your Body Speaks Before Your Mind Knows

Your body is your most reliable intuition tool. It doesn't lie. It doesn't get caught up in spiritual jargon or impressive credentials. It just responds. So start paying attention to what happens physically when you're around a potential teacher.

  • Does your chest feel open and relaxed, or tight and guarded?
  • Do your shoulders drop, or do they creep up toward your ears?
  • Is your breathing deep and easy, or shallow and quick?
  • Do you feel a sense of expansion, or contraction?

These aren't random sensations. They're your nervous system giving you a read on the energetic environment. And here's the key: you don't need to understand why you feel what you feel. You just need to notice it. The "why" can come later. The noticing is what matters right now.

I recommend keeping a simple journal for this. Just a few lines after you encounter a teacher - online or in person. What did your body feel? What was your mood afterward? Did you feel energized and clear, or confused and depleted? Over time, patterns will emerge that your conscious mind could never manufacture.

If you want to go deeper into body-based intuition, The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker is an incredible resource. It's not a spiritual book, but it's one of the best books ever written on how your body detects danger before your mind does. See on Amazon (paid link).

The Second Red Flag: They Make You Feel Small

This one is subtle but devastating. A teacher who makes you feel like you're always "not enough" - not evolved enough, not aware enough, not surrendered enough - is not helping you grow. They're keeping you small so you'll keep coming back for their approval.

Real spiritual teachers will sometimes challenge you. That's part of the deal. But the challenge comes from a place of love and respect, not from a place of superiority. You should feel like they're on your team, even when they're pointing out something hard to hear.

Your intuition knows the difference. When you leave a session or a talk, do you feel inspired and capable? Or do you feel like you need to "fix" yourself before you're worthy of showing up fully? If it's the latter, that's a signal. Don't ignore it.

I've worked with teachers who made me cry - but in a good way. Tears of release, not tears of shame. The difference is night and day. One opens you up. The other closes you down. And your body knows which one is happening.

The Money Test: What Their Relationship with Money Reveals

Money is one of the clearest windows into a teacher's integrity. Not because money is bad, but because how someone handles money reveals their relationship with power, control, and transparency.

Ask yourself: Is their pricing clear and upfront? Do they offer sliding scales or payment plans for those who need them? Do they pressure you into buying more? Do they create a sense of scarcity - "this offer expires soon" - that makes you feel anxious?

Your intuition will often pick up on money dynamics before your rational mind does. That icky feeling when they announce a "special opportunity" that costs thousands? That's your gut saying, "Something's off here."

Here's a simple rule: if a teacher can't be transparent about money, they're probably not transparent about other things either. Spiritual work is real work. It has value. Teachers deserve to be compensated. But the exchange should feel clean, not coercive.

When I first started charging for my work, I was terrified. I thought, "Who am I to ask for money for this?" But I learned that clear boundaries around money are actually an act of respect - for both the teacher and the student. If a teacher can't hold that boundary with integrity, it's a red flag.

How to Test a Teacher Without Being a Skeptic

You don't have to be a cynical jerk to use your intuition. In fact, the most effective approach is to stay open but grounded. Here's a practice I teach my students:

  1. Observe before you commit. Watch their free content. Read their books. Listen to their podcasts. Pay attention to how they talk about other teachers and traditions. Do they speak with respect, even when they disagree? Or do they tear others down?
  2. Notice how their students behave. Do the people who follow them seem empowered and independent? Or do they all sound the same, using the same phrases, defending the same positions? A healthy community produces individuals. A cult produces clones.
  3. Ask a difficult question. Send an email or ask in a live session. Something that challenges their perspective. See how they respond. Do they get defensive? Do they dismiss you? Or do they engage with genuine curiosity? Their response will tell you everything.
  4. Take your time. There is no rush. Any teacher who pushes you to make a decision quickly is not acting in your best interest. Real spiritual growth is patient. It doesn't need to be rushed.

If you're new to working with intuition in a structured way, The Psychic Workbook by Amy Major is a practical guide that can help you build confidence in your own knowing. See on Amazon (paid link).

The Third Red Flag: They Discourage Questions

This might be the biggest one. A teacher who can't handle questions is a teacher who can't handle the truth. If you ask something and they respond with, "You're just not ready for that answer" or "Your doubt is a sign of your own unhealed wounds," that's manipulation, plain and simple.

Real teachers welcome inquiry. They might not have all the answers, but they'll sit with you in the question. They'll say, "I don't know, let's explore that together." That's the mark of someone who's actually walking the path, not just selling tickets to it.

Your intuition will often feel a contraction when a question is dismissed. That sinking feeling in your stomach? That's your soul saying, "This isn't safe." Listen to it.

What to Do When You've Already Invested in the Wrong Teacher

This is the hard one. Maybe you've already given money, time, and emotional energy to someone who turned out to be not what they seemed. Maybe you feel embarrassed. Maybe you feel like you should have known better.

Stop. Breathe. You didn't fail. You learned. And the fact that you're reading this means you're ready to trust yourself more deeply going forward.

Here's what helps: forgive yourself for not knowing what you didn't know. You showed up with an open heart. That's not a weakness. That's courage. The problem wasn't your openness. It was the person who took advantage of it.

Take what was useful from the experience. Maybe you learned something about yourself. Maybe you learned something about spiritual dynamics. Maybe you learned that your intuition was trying to speak, and next time you'll listen sooner. All of that is valuable. None of it is wasted.

If you're working through spiritual trauma or disillusionment, The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk can help you understand how these experiences live in your body and how to release them. See on Amazon (paid link).

Building Your Internal Compass for Good

Here's the truth I want you to walk away with: your intuition is not broken. It's not unreliable. It's been trying to protect you this whole time. The only thing that's been unreliable is your trust in it.

Rebuilding that trust is a practice. It happens one small decision at a time. You start by noticing what your body tells you about a cup of tea. Then a conversation. Then a person. Then a teacher. And over time, you realize that you have everything you need to work through this world - including the spiritual world - without giving your power away.

You don't need to become cynical. You don't need to close your heart. You just need to bring your whole self into the room - including the part that feels things before it understands them. That part is not your enemy. It's your guide.

And the more you listen, the clearer it gets.

If you're ready to go deeper into your own intuitive gifts and learn how to trust them in real time, I've created a free assessment that can help you identify where your natural strengths lie and where you might be second-guessing yourself. Take the intuitive strengths assessment here - it takes about 10 minutes and will give you a clear picture of your unique intuitive profile.
